reason: Part A §2.9 verbatim: Razorpay PA not responsible for refund requests; merchant's sole responsibility.
Part B Part IA §1.1 verbatim defines offline card-present scope AND states Part IA is supplementary to Part A/Part I, not a replacement.
Part A §3.2 verbatim: statutory variations in applicable taxes shall be borne by the merchant.
Part B Part IB §1.19 (LRS) verbatim: merchant must immediately notify Razorpay in writing on any LRS regulatory notice.
Part A §2.1 verbatim: merchant bears unauthorized-access and fraudulent-transaction loss from Secure Credentials compromise; Razorpay not liable.
Part B Part I §1(f) verbatim: Razorpay at sole discretion may recover from Transaction Amount or other held funds.
Part B Part I §7.1 directly answers both parts: Razorpay's Optimizer role is strictly SaaS (not PSP), and merchant indemnifies Razorpay for PG/PA disputes arising from Optimizer.
Part A §9.3 verbatim: forthwith reimbursement of card-network fines plus Razorpay may suspend settlement or suspend/terminate Services forthwith.
Part A §14.3 verbatim: Razorpay may update fees including for previously free services; continued use constitutes consent.
Part A §2.18 verbatim: 10-year retention from the relevant date of order placed on the site.
Part A §3.1 verbatim: Razorpay PA fees include zero MDR for RuPay Debit and UPI transactions.
Part B Part III §1 verbatim: TokenHQ is an end-to-end tokenisation solution compliant with RBI Circular, and the Circular is deemed included in Applicable Laws.
Part A §2.20 verbatim: losses incurred by Razorpay from merchant-imposed convenience fee are borne by merchant, who must also indemnify.
Definitions: Facility Providers verbatim enumerates banks issuing credit/debit cards and Card Networks.
Part A §8.1 verbatim covers both integration-leading-to-data-leakage indemnity scope AND the survive-after-termination covenant.

Refund scenario genuinely forks: customer refund (§2.9, merchant's sole responsibility) vs. Razorpay unilaterally withholding and refunding to source (§1(h)). Two distinct operational regimes; clarification is load-bearing.

Question already states the liability is under a 'separate lending product agreement with one of their Group Entities'. Part A §14.2 directly applies (it expressly covers 'any other agreement between You and Razorpay PA or its Group Entities'). Direct Cat A; the §1(f) branch answers a fact pattern the question didn't describe.

KYC-issue-type axis is load-bearing: §2.2 (inaccurate info → immediate suspend + termination, possibly permanent) vs. §2.8 (non-submission of requested docs → suspend until submission, no termination). Worst-case outcomes materially differ.

Fee-increase-trigger axis is load-bearing for a specific hike: statutory tax (§3.2, no opt-out) vs. Razorpay-priced VAS (§14.3, avoidable by not using the service). A given fee hike falls under one or the other, and the merchant's options differ.

Recovery-trigger axis forks materially: §2.25 penalty (deduction plus separate legal proceedings, no timeline) vs. §2.3 chargeback (two-step with 7-day debit-note deadline). Different process and different deadline.

Question specifically asks about 'our logo... in their promotional materials' — i.e., Razorpay using merchant materials. Direct Cat A under Part A §1.2 (irrevocable license, no incremental consent). The 'merchant using Razorpay's marks' branch (§1.1) is unresponsive to the question.

Branches are concurrent PCI obligations, not alternatives: §12.4 (no storage of card credentials) AND §6.2 (PCI-DSS/PA-DSS compliance + annual report) both apply simultaneously. A direct Cat A listing both satisfies 'what exactly do we need to do'; fork is artificial.

Compliance-issue-type axis forks materially: §3.4 reconciliation discrepancy (liability disclaimer if reported late) vs. §9.3 card-network non-compliance (forthwith reimbursement + suspension/termination). Settlement impact materially different.

Recovery-trigger axis forks materially: §1(c) early-settled tx not received in Escrow within 3 days (absolute forthwith recovery right) vs. §1(f) Facility-Provider-attributable debits (discretionary set-off + shortfall payment obligation).

Cross-border direction axis yields numerically different timelines: PA-CB Inward 15 days (Part B Part I §1.1) vs. PA-CB Outward 25 days (Part B Part IB §1.2). Directly load-bearing for finance planning.

Fee-credit-availability axis load-bearing under §1(e): with credits → fees deducted from credits, full settlement; without → fees deducted from Transaction Amount as Permissible Deduction, reduced settlement. Operationally distinct.

Question specifies 'a fine from a card association that Razorpay says it's going to recover from our settlement' — this fact pattern is exactly §2.25 (penalty imposed on Razorpay by Card Association due to merchant act). Direct Cat A: yes, settlement deduction PLUS separate proceedings. Permissible-Deductions-only branch applies to a scenario that contradicts the question's facts.

Settlement-status axis load-bearing: already-settled → §4.2 RBI-circular dispute process (Razorpay cannot unilaterally claw back) vs. not-yet-settled → Part B Part II Withholding Rights (pre-emptive withholding allowed). Materially different merchant exposure.

Checkout-type axis is drawn directly from Part B Part III Consent clause structure ('You shall, except in the case of standard checkout...'). Standard checkout → Razorpay handles consent; custom → merchant must build explicit-consent UI, share consent during payment flow, and provide de-registration. Operational work differs materially.

Both branches cite the same verbatim §3.1 excerpt and the clause already encodes the condition. Direct Cat A: 'no Razorpay approval needed; you may trigger refunds at your sole discretion, subject to availability of funds in the Escrow Account.' Merchant knows their own escrow balance; this is a factual state the merchant controls, not an ambiguity requiring Razorpay clarification.

Part B Part II Withholding Rights uses purely qualitative 'reasonable grounds to suspect' with no numeric thresholds, velocity limits, or graduated-warning process. Genuine vagueness; answer escalates appropriately.

Part A §9.3 creates forthwith-reimbursement obligation but is entirely silent on contest/audit rights, evidence requirements, dispute mechanism, and proportionality. Genuine silence on procedural safeguards.

Part B Part IV §1 requires both successful AFA and successful first transaction but is silent on the intermediate case (AFA succeeded, first transaction failed) — no retry count, no validity window, no merchant notification rule. Genuine silence.

Part B Part I §4.3 states chargeback is 'resolved in accordance with the Terms' but does not define the precedence hierarchy between Razorpay's Terms and external RBI/NPCI/card-network rules. Genuine external deferral.

Part A §3.2 places tax variations on merchant but is silent on notice period, calculation methodology, cap, retrospective-demand scope, and dispute mechanism. Genuine silence on operational mechanics.
Part A §2.2 uses 'reasonable grounds to suspect' with no threshold, checklist, automated-vs-manual process, cure period, or appeal mechanism defined. Genuine vague language.

Part A §16.2 ties notice obligation to undefined 'Applicable Laws'; silent on wind-down period, in-flight transaction handling, fund-release timeline, and contest/reinstatement rights. Genuine silence.

Part B Part I §4.2 explicitly defers to two named RBI circulars plus 'other notifications... from time to time', without specifying Razorpay's internal recovery mechanics, merchant response timeline, or liability allocation. Classic external deferral.

Part B Part IB §1.5 defers to 'prevailing Indian income tax laws' for rate and applicability, and does not specify whether the Transaction Based Reports satisfy DTAA credit requirements abroad. External deferral on both legs.
Part A §3.1 grants 'sole discretion' with no notice period, no cap, no reasonableness qualifier on fee increases. Genuine silence on merchant protections.

Part A §1.1 restricts derivative works and mark usage but does not define the boundary for white-label implementations, and defers implicitly to RBI PA Guidelines / NPCI rules on branding and disclosure without specifying the interaction. External deferral plus silence.

Part B Part I §1.1 anchors the 5-day clock to 'receipt of Transaction Amount in the Escrow Account' but does not define 'receipt' when funds arrive in partial tranches. Defers to RBI PA Guidelines without reproducing them. Genuine silence.

Part A §14.3 treats continued use as consent but does not specify advance-notice period or cap, and does not explain how RBI PA Guidelines / NPCI circulars constrain this discretion. Genuine external deferral.

Part A §3.7 is silent on post-invoice remediation when GSTIN was outdated at invoice time — no credit-note, amended-invoice, or GSTR rectification obligation is specified. The ToS only grants Razorpay recovery rights, no corresponding merchant-remedy obligation. Genuine silence deferring to CGST Act.

Part A §8.1 requires indemnification of 'reasonable attorneys' fees' without defining a benchmark, cap, itemization duty, or dispute mechanism. Genuine vague-language ambiguity with appropriate escalation.
